---
title: "Tutorial: Using a Hidden Markov Model to Predict Behavioral Epochs with Deep Learning Features"
editor: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
---

## Overview

This tutorial walks through a complete workflow for classifying
behavioral epochs in video data using deep learning feature extraction
and a Hidden Markov Model (HMM). The pipeline is:

1.  **Extract per-frame features** — Run a pre-trained encoder (e.g.
    ConvNeXt \[[paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2201.03545)\]) over each
    video frame to produce a compact feature vector.
2.  **Reduce dimensionality** — Apply post-encoder processing (global
    average pooling or spatial point extraction) to collapse the spatial
    feature map into a 1D feature vector per frame.
3.  **Train an HMM** — Use the feature time series and a set of labeled
    epochs to fit a Hidden Markov Model that captures the temporal
    structure of behavioral states.
4.  **Predict epochs** — Run the trained HMM on unlabeled frames to
    automatically segment the video into behavioral epochs.

By the end you will have a repeatable pipeline that turns raw video into
labeled behavioral intervals stored as `DigitalIntervalSeries` in the
DataManager.

## Prerequisites

-   A video loaded in the Media Viewer.
-   A pre-trained encoder model exported as `.pt2` (AOT Inductor) or
    `.pt` (TorchScript). See [Exporting an AOTI Encoder
    Model](exporting_aoti_encoder.qmd) for how to export a ConvNeXt
    backbone.
-   (Optional) Labeled behavioral intervals for training the HMM. These
    can be created using the [Label Video
    Intervals](label_video_intervals.qmd) workflow.

## Pipeline Overview

```         
Video Frames
     │
     ▼
┌─────────────┐
│   Encoder   │  ConvNeXt / ViT / ResNet
│  (forward)  │  [B, 3, 224, 224] → [B, 768, 7, 7]
└─────┬───────┘
      │
      ▼
┌─────────────────────┐
│ Post-Encoder Module │  Global Avg Pool or Spatial Point Extraction
│                     │  [B, 768, 7, 7] → [B, 768]
└─────┬───────────────┘
      │
      ▼
┌─────────────┐
│ TensorData  │  One 768-dim feature vector per frame
│ (stored in  │  stored in DataManager
│ DataManager)│
└─────┬───────┘
      │
      ▼
┌─────────────┐
│    HMM      │  Trained on labeled intervals
│  (predict)  │  Outputs state sequence
└─────┬───────┘
      │
      ▼
┌───────────────-───────┐
│ DigitalIntervalSeries │  Behavioral epoch labels
│ (stored in            │  per frame
│  DataManager)         │
└───────────────────────┘
```

## Step 1: Export Your Encoder Model

Follow the [Exporting an AOTI Encoder Model](exporting_aoti_encoder.qmd)
how-to guide to export a ConvNeXt-Tiny backbone. You will end up with:

-   `convnext_encoder.pt2` — the compiled model weights
-   `convnext_encoder.json` — the `RuntimeModelSpec` that describes the
    model's I/O

## Step 2: Load the Encoder in Neuralyzer

*This section will be expanded once the GeneralEncoderModel and
post-encoder modules are implemented.*

1.  Open **View → Tools → Deep Learning**.
2.  Load the `convnext_encoder.json` model spec.
3.  Browse to the `.pt2` weights file.
4.  Bind the `"image"` input slot to your video media data.
5.  Configure a post-encoder module (Global Average Pooling) to reduce
    the feature map.
6.  Bind the output to a `TensorData` key (e.g. `"encoder_features"`).

## Step 3: Extract Features Over the Video

*This section will be expanded once batch inference with post-encoder
modules is wired up.*

Run batch inference over all frames (or a frame range). Each frame
produces a feature vector stored in the `"encoder_features"` TensorData
object.

## Step 4: Label Training Epochs

If you don't already have labeled behavioral intervals:

1.  Use the [Label Video Intervals](label_video_intervals.qmd) workflow
    to mark epochs of distinct behaviors (e.g. "grooming", "resting",
    "locomotion").
2.  Store them as `DigitalIntervalSeries` objects in the DataManager.

These labeled intervals serve as the training signal for the HMM.

## Step 5: Train the HMM

*This section will be expanded once the MLCore HMM integration is
available.*

## Step 6: Predict Behavioral Epochs

*This section will be expanded once the HMM prediction pipeline is
complete.*

## Next Steps

-   Evaluate prediction accuracy using held-out labeled data.
-   Experiment with different encoders (ViT, ResNet) and post-encoder
    modules (spatial point extraction at a landmark location vs. global
    average pooling).
-   Fine-tune the number of HMM states and observation model.
